tagline: Calculate the hourly rate you need to charge as a freelancer

ogTitle: Freelance Rate Calculator — Your Ideal Hourly Rate

jsonLdWeb.name: Freelance Rate Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate your freelance hourly rate based on desired salary, expenses, time off, and billable hours.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate my freelance hourly rate?

acceptedAnswer.text: Add your desired annual salary plus annual business expenses (insurance, software, taxes, etc.). Divide by the number of billable hours per year. Billable hours = (work days minus vacation minus sick days) times 8 hours times billable percentage. Add a profit margin on top.

Include: self-employment tax (15.3% in the US), health insurance, retirement contributions, software subscriptions, equipment, office rent or coworking, accounting fees, professional development, marketing costs, and a buffer for irregular income.

mainEntity.name: What percentage of time is billable for freelancers?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most freelancers are billable only 60-70% of their working time. The rest goes to marketing, admin, invoicing, prospecting, emails, and professional development. New freelancers may be billable only 40-50% of the time while building a client base.

mainEntity.name: Should I charge hourly or project-based rates?

acceptedAnswer.text: Knowing your hourly rate is essential even if you charge per project. Use your hourly rate to estimate project costs and ensure profitability. Project-based pricing is often better because it rewards efficiency and clients prefer predictable costs. But your hourly rate is the foundation.

mainEntity.name: How much should freelancers charge compared to employees?

acceptedAnswer.text: Freelancers typically need to charge 30-50% more than the equivalent employee hourly rate. This covers self-employment tax, health insurance, retirement, no paid time off, equipment costs, and business expenses that employers normally pay.

mainEntity.name: How do I raise my freelance rate?

acceptedAnswer.text: Raise rates for new clients first, then gradually for existing ones with 30-60 days notice. Specialize in a niche, demonstrate ROI, and build a strong portfolio. Most freelancers undercharge in their first 1-2 years. Review and adjust rates at least annually.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Your Freelance Rate

Your freelance rate needs to cover much more than just your take-home pay. Unlike employees, freelancers pay for their own taxes, insurance, equipment, and have no guaranteed income.

Hourly Rate = (Salary + Expenses) × (1 + Profit%) / Billable Hours
  • Billable hours = (260 work days − vacation − sick) × 8 hrs × billable %
  • Most freelancers bill 60–70% of their working hours
  • Include a profit margin for growth, savings, and income fluctuation
  • Factor in self-employment tax (~15.3% in the US) in your expenses

Example: $80K salary + $15K expenses, 15 vacation days, 30% non-billable, 10% profit → $77/hr
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How do I calculate my freelance hourly rate?

Add your desired annual salary to your annual business expenses (insurance, software, self-employment taxes, equipment, etc.). Divide the total by your billable hours per year. Billable hours equals working days minus time off, times 8 hours, times your billable percentage. Add a profit margin on top for growth and income fluctuation.

What expenses should freelancers include?

Include self-employment tax (15.3% in the US), health insurance, retirement contributions, software subscriptions, equipment and computer costs, office rent or coworking fees, accounting and legal fees, professional development, marketing costs, and a buffer for irregular income months.

What percentage of time is billable for freelancers?

Most established freelancers bill 60–70% of their working time. The remaining 30–40% goes to marketing, administration, invoicing, client prospecting, email, and professional development. New freelancers may be billable only 40–50% while building their client base.

Should I charge hourly or project-based rates?

Knowing your hourly rate is essential even if you quote per project. Use your hourly rate to estimate project costs and ensure profitability. Project-based pricing is often better for both parties — it rewards your efficiency and gives clients predictable costs. Your hourly rate is the foundation for all pricing.

How much should freelancers charge compared to employees?

Freelancers typically need to charge 30–50% more than the equivalent employee hourly rate. This premium covers self-employment tax, health insurance, retirement, no paid time off, equipment costs, and all the business expenses that employers normally absorb for their employees.

How do I raise my freelance rate?

Start by raising rates for new clients first, then gradually increase for existing clients with 30–60 days written notice. Specialize in a niche, demonstrate clear ROI for clients, and build a strong portfolio. Most freelancers significantly undercharge in their first one to two years. Review and adjust rates at least annually.

howWeCalculate.intro: Your rate has to cover the salary you want, the business costs an employer would normally pay for you, and the hours you work but never invoice. The calculator adds those up and spreads them across your real billable hours.
steps: Add your desired take-home income to your yearly business costs — self-employment tax, health insurance, retirement, software, and equipment.
Count your real billable hours: start from 260 weekdays, subtract vacation and sick days, multiply by 8, then keep only the share you can bill (usually 60–70%).
Multiply the total cost by your profit margin, then divide by those billable hours to get the rate you need on every billed hour.
Read the hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly figures alongside the total revenue you have to book to reach your target.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These are planning estimates. Self-employment tax, deductions, and benefit costs vary by country and situation — confirm your tax and insurance numbers with an accountant before you lock in rates.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this whenever a pricing number is on the line and you want it grounded in what you actually need to earn.
scenarios: You're leaving a salaried job and need the hourly rate that replaces that paycheck once you're paying your own taxes and benefits.
You've been busy but broke, and you suspect your current rate doesn't cover the non-billable half of your week.
A client asks you to quote a 40-hour project and you need a price that still profits after overhead and revisions.
You're planning next year's income and want to know how many billable hours it takes to get there.
You're weighing a freelance contract against a full-time salary and need an honest apples-to-apples hourly figure.

sections.title: What This Calculator Actually Does
sections.body: This tool turns a take-home target into the hourly rate you need to quote. You enter the income you want, your yearly business costs, your days off, and the share of your week that never gets billed — it returns your hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly rate plus the total revenue you have to book to hit your goal. The thing most people underprice on: your invoice rate is not your salary divided by 2,080 hours, because you don't get to bill anywhere near 2,080 hours.

sections.title: Why Your Rate Lands So Much Higher Than $40
sections.body: Say you want to take home $80,000 with about $15,000 of yearly costs, 20 days off, and 30% of your week spent on marketing, admin, and invoicing. You bill roughly 1,340 hours, not 2,080, so you need about $78 an hour — not the $38 you'd get from $80,000 divided by 2,080. Trim your billable time toward 1,000 hours, or add self-employment tax and insurance on top, and that rate climbs past $100. A $40 salaried wage and a $40 freelance rate are not the same paycheck.

sections.title: Billable Hours Are the Number That Bites
sections.body: Even a full-time freelancer rarely bills more than 60–70% of their working hours; the rest goes to finding clients, sending invoices, answering email, and learning. In your first year that billable share can drop to 40–50% while you build a pipeline. This calculator makes you name that percentage, because it's the single input that swings your rate the most — dropping from 70% to 50% billable raises the rate you need by nearly a third.

howToSteps.title: Enter the income you want to take home
howToSteps.description: Put in the annual pay you're aiming for before income tax — the number that would land in your pocket as salary if this were a job.

howToSteps.title: Add your yearly costs and time off
howToSteps.description: Fill in business expenses (self-employment tax, insurance, software, equipment), then your vacation and sick days and the share of the week that isn't billable.

howToSteps.title: Set a profit margin and calculate
howToSteps.description: Add a margin for slow months and growth, then hit Calculate to run the numbers.

howToSteps.title: Read your rate across every unit
howToSteps.description: Check the hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly rate plus your billable hours and the total revenue you need — use them to quote work and plan the year.

tips: Put self-employment tax (~15.3% in the US) and health insurance into the expenses field — leaving them out is the most common way freelancers end up underpricing.
Be honest about non-billable time. If you're new, marketing and admin can eat 40–50% of your week, not 30%.
Quote per project when you can, but use the hourly rate here to build the price — estimate the hours, multiply, then add margin for scope creep.
Raise the rate on new clients first; give existing clients 30–60 days' notice before an increase takes effect.
Rerun the numbers every year. If your billable hours dropped or your costs rose, your rate should move with them.

faq.question: Why is my freelance rate so much higher than an employee's hourly wage?
faq.answer: Because you pay for everything an employer used to cover. On top of your take-home target you carry self-employment tax (~15.3% in the US), health insurance, retirement, equipment, and unpaid time off — and you only bill 60–70% of your hours. A $40/hr salaried job often maps to a $70–$90/hr freelance rate for the same take-home pay.

faq.question: How many hours a year can I actually bill?
faq.answer: Far fewer than 2,080. Start from 260 weekdays, subtract 15–25 days off, then keep only the 60–70% of the rest that isn't marketing, admin, invoicing, or email. That usually leaves 1,100–1,400 billable hours — and closer to 1,000 in your first year while you're finding clients.

faq.question: Do I add taxes to my rate, or pay them out of it?
faq.answer: You build them in. Freelancers owe self-employment tax plus income tax, and no one withholds it for you. Put your estimated self-employment tax in the expenses field so the rate you charge already covers it, then set aside roughly 25–30% of income for tax time.

faq.question: What should I count as a business expense?
faq.answer: Self-employment tax, health insurance, retirement contributions, software and subscriptions, computer and equipment, coworking or home-office costs, accounting and legal fees, professional development, and marketing. Add a buffer for months when work is thin — that's a real cost too.

faq.question: Should I charge hourly or by the project?
faq.answer: Quote projects when you can — clients like a fixed price, and you keep the upside when you work fast. But price the project off the hourly rate here: estimate the hours, multiply, and add margin for revisions. The hourly number is the floor you don't want to drop below.

faq.question: How do I raise my rates without losing clients?
faq.answer: Raise the rate on new clients right away, since they have no old number to anchor to. For existing clients, give 30–60 days' written notice and tie the increase to results you've delivered. Most freelancers undercharge for a year or two, so an annual review is normal, not aggressive.
